title: Psalms 76
subtitle: In Judah God Is Known

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “His Tent Is in Salem, His Dwelling Place in Zion”

heb.paragraph: "His tent is in Salem (shālēm), his dwelling place in Zion" (v.2). Salem = Jerusalem (Gen 14:18). God’s dwelling is in the city of peace. There he broke the weapons of war: arrows, shields, swords (v.3). "You are radiant with light, more majestic than mountains rich with game" (v.4). The warriors were plundered, they slept their last sleep (v.5). At God’s rebuke, horse and chariot lie still (v.6).

notes.note: "In Judah God is known. His tent is in Salem, his dwelling place in Zion. There he broke the flashing arrows, the shields and the swords." God’s home is where war ends. Weapons are broken in his presence.
"The valiant lie plundered, they sleep their last sleep. At your rebuke, the horse and chariot lie still." One rebuke from God ends all military might. The most powerful armies become still.

refs.note: The angel striking 185,000 Assyrians may be the historical event behind this psalm's celebration.
The parallel account of Sennacherib's defeat confirms the sudden divine intervention the psalm celebrates.
The rider on the white horse judging and making war fulfills the psalm's divine warrior imagery.

hist.context: Psalm 76 is a Zion song celebrating God's dwelling in Jerusalem and his defeat of attacking armies. The reference to Salem (v.2, an ancient name for Jerusalem, cf. Gen 14:18) emphasizes the city's sacred antiquity. The broken weapons — shield, sword, and implements of war (v.3) — suggest a specific military deliverance, possibly Sennacherib's failed siege of 701 BC.

sections.header: Verses 7–12 — “Make Vows to the LORD Your God and Fulfil Them”

heb.paragraph: "You alone are to be feared (nōrāʾ). Who can stand before you when you are angry?" (v.7). From heaven God pronounced judgment; the earth feared and was quiet (v.8). God arose to save all the afflicted of the land (v.9). "Surely your wrath against mankind brings you praise" (v.10). The nations are commanded: make vows and fulfil them. "He breaks the spirit of rulers; he is feared by the kings of the earth" (v.12).

notes.note: "You alone are to be feared. From heaven you pronounced judgment; the earth feared and was quiet." Divine judgment produces cosmic silence. When God speaks, everything else is still.
"Surely your wrath against mankind brings you praise." Even God’s wrath serves his glory. Judgment becomes praise — because justice is revealed.
"He breaks the spirit of rulers; he is feared by the kings of the earth." Political power bows to divine power. Every ruler is subject to God’s judgment.

refs.note: Coming to see God's desolations and ceasing wars parallels this psalm's battlefield imagery.
Nations worshiping from their own lands develops the psalm's vision of universal awe.
Every knee bowing fulfills the psalm's portrayal of rulers humbled before God.

hist.context: The declaration that God 'breaks the spirit of rulers' and is 'feared by the kings of the earth' (v.12) inverts ancient power dynamics. The psalm instructs surrounding nations to bring tribute to 'the One to be feared' (v.11). This theology of divine sovereignty over nations shaped Israel's understanding of international politics as ultimately subject to YHWH.

ppl.text: " One rebuke from God ends all military might…

rec.text: ""In Judah God is known. His tent is in Salem, his dwelling place in Zion. There he broke the flashing arrows, the shields and the swords." God’s home is where war ends. Weapons are broken in his prese…"

rows.label: The Earth Was Quiet — 76:7–12
rows.text: "You alone are to be feared." Heaven speaks; earth is silent. "He breaks the spirit of rulers."

hebtext.note: "His tent is in Salem (shālēm), his dwelling place in Zion" (v.2). Salem = Jerusalem (Gen 14:18). God’s dwelling is in the city of peace. There he broke the weapons of war: arrows, shields, swords (v.3). "You are radiant with light, more majestic than mountains rich with game" (v.4). The warriors were plundered, they slept their last sleep (v.5). At God’s rebuke, horse and chariot lie still (v.6).

"You alone are to be feared (nōrāʾ). Who can stand before you when you are angry?" (v.7). From heaven God pronounced judgment; the earth feared and was quiet (v.8). God arose to save all the afflicted of the land (v.9). "Surely your wrath against mankind brings you praise" (v.10). The nations are commanded: make vows and fulfil them. "He breaks the spirit of rulers; he is feared by the kings of the earth" (v.12).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: one rebuke stills armies. Holiness: "you alone are to be feared." Judgment: rulers broken, the earth silenced.

prayer_prompt: This psalm celebrates God's power displayed in battle for His people. Consider asking God where you need to remember that He fights for you — and where your confidence has shifted from His strength to your strategies.
